data.title: Taitron to voluntary delist from Nasdaq
data.description:
• Taitron Components (TAIT) on Friday reported Q3 EPS of -$0.01 and Net product revenue of $529,000.
  • The company declared cash dividend of $0.035 per common share.
  • Taitron also announced the resignation of its CFO David Vanderhorst, effective immediately.
  • Additionally, the company said that it will voluntarily delist its common stock from Nasdaq and suspend its public reporting obligations.
  • Taitron said that its costs of maintaining the Nasdaq listing and remaining a public reporting company, including costs of compliance, the demands on management time and the company resources required to maintain its listed and registered status, outweigh the benefits to the company and its stockholders of continued Nasdaq listing and SEC reporting.
  • It will file a Form 25 with the Securities and Exchange Commission on or about November 24, 2025, and the Nasdaq delisting is expected to become effective on or about December 4, 2025, at which time trading on Nasdaq will cease.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Fed's not moving in direction of goals on both mandates, Bostic says
data.description:
Federal Reserve Bank of Atlanta President Raphael Bostic said Friday that on both of the central bank's mandates -- full employment and stable prices -- "we are not moving in a direction that's getting us to target."

He reiterated that the Fed faces difficult trade-offs between supporting employment and controlling inflation.

And the recent lack of official data because of the government shutdown makes it "an incredibly challenging environment to be doing policy," Bostic said in a fireside chat at an industry conference.

Bostic, who recently announced plans to retire at the end of his current term in February 2026, noted that he was able to support the Fed's last two rate cuts. He said he hopes the December meeting will be accompanied by "a lot of data" to properly assess the policy path.

data.title: Capital Bancorp appoints Jacob Dalaya as CFO
data.description:
• Capital Bancorp (NASDAQ:CBNK)  announced on Friday the appointment of Jacob Dalaya as Executive Vice President and CFO, effective immediately.
  • Dalaya brings in his vast expertise in strategic and financial planning and in-depth knowledge of the industry.
  • The company has $3.4 billion in assets as of September 30, 2025.

data.title: Philippe Laffont's Coatue takes new stake in Applied Materials, cuts position in CoreWeave in Q3 moves
data.description:
Billionaire investor Philippe Laffont's Coatue Management in the third quarter picked up a new stake in Applied Materials (AMAT), significantly reduced its position in CoreWeave (CRWV), while exiting Philip Morris International (PM), among other changes.

Coatue disclosed the information in its latest 13F filing, which was published on Friday.

The fund exited its stake in Philip Morris International (PM), selling 1.29M shares, while it sold 117,415 shares of pharma giant Eli Lilly (LLY). Other exits include Yum China (YUMC), Generac Holdings (GNRC) and LexinFintech Holdings (LX).

Some new stakes in the quarter include 1.8M shares in Synopsys (SNPS), 3.34M shares in Applied Materials (AMAT), 1.95M shares of Snowflake (SNOW), 2.09M Class C shares of Alphabet (GOOG) and 3.23M shares of Figma (FIG).

The fund raised its stake in Oracle (ORCL) to 4.79M shares from 3.02M shares. Another stock where it significantly raised from 2.01M to 7.22M shares is Alphabet Class A holdings (GOOGL). The fund also raised its stake in Microsoft (MSFT), AppLovin (APP) and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM).

Coatue, known for its investment in tech-heavy companies, slashed its position in Amazon (AMZN) from 10.1M shares to 8.74M shares, while it cut its stake in Arm Holdings (ARM) by 2.37M shares to 2.25M shares.

The most noticeable reduction in stake was in AI infrastructure provider CoreWeave (CRWV), where the position was reduced from 17.8M Class A shares to 6.72M shares.

data.title: Druckenmiller's Duquesne takes in Amazon, Google, Meta, exits Microsoft, Lilly in Q3 moves
data.description:
Billionaire investor Stanley Druckenmiller's Duquesne Family Office picked up a new stake in tech giants Amazon (AMZN), Meta Platforms (META), and Alphabet (GOOG)(GOOGL) in Q3 2025, while exiting its position in major names such as Microsoft (MSFT) and Eli Lilly (LLY), among other moves.

The investment firm disclosed the information in its latest 13F filing for the three months ended September 30, 2025, published on Friday.

Duquesne snapped up 437K shares in Amazon (AMZN) and 76K class A shares in the Facebook owner. It also took a new stake of 102K class A shares (GOOGL) in the Google parent. Additionally, the fund took in 77K shares in energy company GE Vernova (GEV).

Turning to exits, aside from Microsoft (MSFT) and Lilly (LLY), Druckenmiller's firm also got rid of its holdings in cigarette maker Philip Morris International (PM) and in semiconductor materials suppliers Entegris (ENTG) and Coherent (COHR). 

Looking at stake increases, Duquesne upped its position in Israeli generic drugmaker Teva Pharmaceutical Industries (TEVA) by 3.9% to 16.6M American depositary shares. It also nearly tripled its position in the number two U.S. lender by assets, Bank of America (BAC).

Finally, notable stake decreases include a 28.6% reduction to 895K shares in energy firm EQT (EQT). 

According to Forbes, Stanley Druckenmiller's real-time net worth is $7.8B.

  • David Tepper's Appaloosa takes new AMD stake, exits Intel, among top Q3 trades

data.title: Hammond appoints Paul Heuts as CFO
data.description:
• Hammond Manufacturing Company (OTCPK:HMFAF) said on Friday it has appointed Paul Heuts as Chief Financial Officer effective November 17, replacing interim CFO Alex Stirling, who will return to his role as Executive Vice President.
  • Heuts previously served as Vice President of finance for Mattr’s Connection Technologies group and brings 18 years of experience in finance and manufacturing operations.
  • HMFAF closed -7.76% at $8.0189.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Wall Street ends mixed, Nasdaq survives from selloff amid valuation worries
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages closed mixed, with the Nasdaq Composite ((COMP:IND)) scrawling up, although ending the week with loses after investors sold off over worries about lofty valuations.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) closed -0.1%, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) ended +0.1%, and the Dow (DJI) finished -0.7%.

Both the S&P 500 ((SP500)) and the Dow ((DJI)) added to weekly gains -- +0.1% and +0.3%, respectively -- while the Nasdaq ((COMP:IND)) ended the week -0.5%.

“Investors’ experience of the market this week was determined by whether they owned high-beta growth names or a more regular collection of workaday equities, bonds, ETFs and whatnot. The grownups experience of this week has been ‘meh, a garden variety spot of weakness’; basement-dwelling degenerates everywhere are thinking about how to explain to their Moms that they are going to need more allowance next month, on account of what has happened,” Seeking Alpha analyst Alex King of Cestrian Capital Research said.

“Everyone has to learn this lesson once; if you were burned in 2021 you probably wised up already. If this is your first rodeo, congratulations, you have now paid tuition fees.  We continue to look higher into year-end,” King added.

Four out of the seven “Magnificent” stocks ended in the red, including Alphabet (GOOG) -0.9%, Amazon (AMZN) -1.3%, Apple (AAPL) -0.3%, and Meta (META) -0.1%.

Financials (XLF) was one of the sectors lagging the most, with big bank names such as Goldman Sachs (GS), American Express (AXP), Visa (V), and JPMorgan Chase (JPM) among the top decliners within the Dow ((DJI)).

Adding to investors' worries, odds of a December 25-basis-point interest rate cut fell to 45.9%, according to the CME FedWatch tool.

Also, economic releases that had been delayed due to the U.S. government shutdown could start to trickle down next week, giving investors and the Fed a lot to ponder on before the December monetary policy meeting.

On crypto, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is now below $95,000, reaching bear market territory.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 2 basis points to 4.15%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.62%.

Wall Street's major indexes closed significantly lower on Thursday as heightened market sentiment due to the end of the U.S. government shutdown dissipated, and focus was turned to the economy.
